description: User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

When a hot-patch file download is triggered using the switch WebUI, log messages can incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.
For Classifier policy modifications to be secure, Aruba strongly encourages modifications be done as a three-step process: Bring down the port, modify, and bring the port back up.
Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.
No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

feature: DHCP Server, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Snooping
description: DHCP Relay and DHCP Snooping can co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping and DHCP Server cannot co-exist on the same switch. DHCP Snooping, DHCP Relay, and DHCP Server together cannot co-exist on the same switch.

Reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services within the system are not recommended to be used for other services. When two services use the same port there is chance of unexpected behaviors from these services. Best practice is to use unique port for each service across system.
Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.
REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

Symptom: ERSPAN mirroring on 6100/4100i switches fails to capture TX (egress) traffic from remote switches. Only RX traffic and locally-originated TX traffic are mirrored to the ERSPAN destination. ICMP requests from remote switches are missing in the mirrored GRE traffic, while replies are captured correctly. Scenario: This issue occurs when ERSPAN mirroring is configured on a 6100 or 4100i switch and the GRE tunnel egress port is the same as the port where the original packet ingressed. For example, when traffic arrives on port 1/1/3 from a remote switch and needs to be mirrored via GRE tunnel that also exits through port 1/1/3, the mirror fails due to ingress port filtering. Workaround: Connect the ERSPAN tunnel destination (mirror endpoint) via a separate physical port than the mirrored source uplink port, ensuring the tunnel traffic exits through a different port than where the original packet was received.
symptom: ERSPAN mirroring on 6100/4100i switches fails to capture TX (egress) traffic from remote switches. Only RX traffic and locally-originated TX traffic are mirrored to the ERSPAN destination. ICMP requests from remote switches are missing in the mirrored GRE traffic, while replies are captured correctly.

For example, when traffic arrives on port 1/1/3 from a remote switch and needs to be mirrored via GRE tunnel that also exits through port 1/1/3, the mirror fails due to ingress port filtering.
workaround: Connect the ERSPAN tunnel destination (mirror endpoint) via a separate physical port than the mirrored source uplink port, ensuring the tunnel traffic exits through a different port than where the original packet was received.

description: Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur when resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.
symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device.
scenario: This issue can occur when resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports.
workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

description: Symptom: ACL logging displays incorrect ACE sequence number and action while entries are being updated in hardware. Scenario: This issue occurs when an ACL entry is configured with the logging action the logged output may report an incorrect sequence number and action. Workaround: Wait for ACL programming to finish before utilizing logs for debug/analysis of traffic.
symptom: ACL logging displays incorrect ACE sequence number and action while entries are being updated in hardware.
scenario: This issue occurs when an ACL entry is configured with the logging action the logged output may report an incorrect sequence number and action.
workaround: Wait for ACL programming to finish before utilizing logs for debug/analysis of traffic.

description: Symptom: Management access (SSH, Ping, Web UI) blocked when MAC ACL permit entries include 'log' option on 6000 switch series. Scenario: This issue may occur when MAC ACL is configured with 'permit' and 'log' actions applied to interface on 6000 switch series.
symptom: Management access (SSH, Ping, Web UI) blocked when MAC ACL permit entries include 'log' option on 6000 switch series.
scenario: This issue may occur when MAC ACL is configured with 'permit' and 'log' actions applied to interface on 6000 switch series.

description: Symptom: Port flapping with port-access authentication cycling and causes STP instability. Ports continuously transition between blocking/forwarding states. LLDP PVID mismatches cause neighbor relationship instability and device profile classification failures. Scenario: This issue may occur when device-profile authentication is enabled with LLDP-based switch classification and port security + STP is enabled.
symptom: Port flapping with port-access authentication cycling and causes STP instability. Ports continuously transition between blocking/forwarding states. LLDP PVID mismatches cause neighbor relationship instability and device profile classification failures.
scenario: This issue may occur when device-profile authentication is enabled with LLDP-based switch classification and port security + STP is enabled.

description: Symptom: High CPU utilization from PTP daemon despite PTP being disabled, causing VSF reboots with Critical Service Fault. Scenario: This issue may occur after a system boot/restart, and the interface state changes on VSF stack members, leading to an agent crash/restart during the operation.
symptom: High CPU utilization from PTP daemon despite PTP being disabled, causing VSF reboots with Critical Service Fault.
scenario: This issue may occur after a system boot/restart, and the interface state changes on VSF stack members, leading to an agent crash/restart during the operation.

description: Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

description: Symptom: STP ports are going into a Disabled/Blocking state. Scenario: This issue can occur after an edge device logs off and logs in back on a secured port or when a server connected to a VSX pair is rebooted. Workaround: Reboot the PVST process.
symptom: STP ports are going into a Disabled/Blocking state.
scenario: This issue can occur after an edge device logs off and logs in back on a secured port or when a server connected to a VSX pair is rebooted.
